comment: Near-identical worked examples (mass-noun stuff/puddle-pile examples) and near-identical unity criterion (mereological invariance / topological self-connectedness); both are endurant/substantial-side categories. The closest structural pairing in this crosswalk.

Both are physical/concrete, endurant-equivalent (Presential) categories with parallel naming ("amount of matter"/"amount of substrate"), but GFO's class has no prose definition to compare against DOLCE's detailed gloss — not `high` for that reason.
Both endurant/substantial-side, physical-stuff categories; same caveat as `mapping-002` about GFO's missing prose definition.

Same subject-matter label but a genuine top-level classification divergence: DOLCE files it as a physical-endurant; SUMO files it as Abstract, defined by measurement/specification rather than physical stuff-hood (SUMO's own "need not be material" clause). Not `close-match` because the classification difference is structural, per `note-001`.

SUMO's "how many/how much" measurement-centric framing is closer in kind to YAMATO's own unit-independent, value-like "true quantity" than either is to DOLCE's/UFO's mass-noun-stuff sense — but SUMO's Quantity still covers Number and PhysicalQuantity broadly, while YAMATO's is embedded in a specific quality/quale theory, so `related`, not `close-match`.
Recorded explicitly as a non-equivalence, not a correspondence, despite the identical English label — see `note-002`. Naive name-matching would incorrectly treat these as the same concept.
Recorded explicitly as a type mismatch (relation vs. entity class), not a correspondence — see `note-003`.
No BFO correspondence identified; zero occurrences of "quantity"/"amount" anywhere in `bfo-core.ttl`.
No reified TUpper correspondence identified; `amount`/`mass`/`constitutes` are informal-semantics predicates relating a value to a material object, not a stuff-denoting class.
